steps.prompt: Which word is the finite verb?

hints.Ierusalem: 'Ierusalem' is a proper noun (indeclinable) — the city being addressed
hints.quae: 'Quae' is a relative pronoun introducing the relative clause
hints.prophetas: 'Prophetas' is a noun meaning 'prophets' in the accusative plural

hints.to fall: 'To fall' is 'occidere' (with short -i-) from 'ob + cadere' — 'occidis' here is from 'occidere' (to kill)
hints.to hide: 'To hide' is 'occultare' or 'abscondere' — not related to 'occidere'

hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'occidisti' — 'occidis' has the present stem
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'occidas' — 'occidis' ends in -is (indicative)
hints.future indicative: Future would be 'occides' — 'occidis' has the present ending -is

hints.occido, occidere, occidi, occasum: These are the parts of the other 'occidere' meaning 'to fall/set' (of the sun)
hints.cado, cadere, cecidi, casum: These are the principal parts of 'cadere' (to fall) — the simple verb
hints.caedo, caedere, cecidi, caesum: These are the principal parts of 'caedere' (to cut/strike)

hints.nominative: Nominative plural would be 'prophetae' — 'prophetas' with -as is accusative plural
hints.genitive: Genitive singular would be 'prophetae' — 'prophetas' is accusative plural
hints.ablative: Ablative plural would be 'prophetis' — 'prophetas' ends in -as

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'prophetae' ends in -ae
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'prophetae' ends in -ae
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'prophetae' ends in -ae

steps.prompt: What is the grammatical function of 'quae'?

hints.interrogative pronoun: An interrogative would introduce a question — 'quae' here introduces a relative clause
hints.indefinite pronoun: An indefinite 'quae' would mean 'some' — here it is a relative pronoun after 'Ierusalem'
hints.demonstrative pronoun: Demonstrative pronouns are 'hic, ille, is' — 'quae' is relative

hints.scribal copying error: The repetition is deliberate — a rhetorical device of emphatic address
hints.it names two different cities: Both refer to the same city — the repetition expresses grief
hints.apposition with different cases: 'Ierusalem' is indeclinable — both instances are vocative

translation: Jerusalem, Jerusalem, who kills the prophets
